coredata.dc:title: Continuous monitoring of temperature and freshness in cold chain transport based on the dual-responsive fluorescent hydrogel
coredata.dc:creator: Cao, Yiran
Chen, Mengting
Li, Jialin
Liu, Weipeng
Zhu, Hongshuai
Liu, Yingju
coredata.dc:description: Comprehensive attention should be paid to the potential food spoilage in food transport. However, there is a problem of freshness destruction by repeated freezing and thawing during the cold chain transport. Herein, a fluorescent hydrogel with N-doped green-emitting carbon dots (N-GCDs), bovine serum albumin-gold nanoclusters (BSA-AuNCs) as fluorescent probes and polyvinyl alcohol-sodium alginate hydrogel as carrier matrix was developed to continuously detect temperature and freshness. Due to the solvatochromic effect of N-GCDs, when the temperature surpassed the threshold, the mixture of water and dimethyl sulfoxide underwent a phase transition and melted into the gel, changing the fluorescence color to realize the temperature monitoring. Then, due to the pH effect of BSA-AuNCs, the gel could respond to pH changes in food deterioration to monitor the food freshness. Thus, the changes of both fluorescence color and intensity of the hydrogel provides a new method for visual and portable authenticity of food freshness.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Solvatochromic effect
Transport temperature
Food freshness
Fluorescent hydrogels

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the N-GCDs/BSA-AuNCs/PVA-SA fluorescent hydrogel
Selection of H2O/DMSO mixture ratio to the threshold temperature
Study on the feasibility of monitoring fish freshness with the fluorescent hydrogel
Study on the threshold temperature at different H2O/DMSO ratio
Monitoring temperature changes based on the N-GCDs/BSA-AuNCs/PVA-SA fluorescent hydrogel
Monitoring the fish freshness by the N-GCDs/BSA-AuNCs/PVA-SA fluorescent hydrogel

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22274059 , 32170100 ), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation ( 2022M711058 ), and Natural Science Foundation of Henan Province ( 232300420195 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM and (B) HRTEM image of N-GCDs. (C) FT-IR spectra of N-GCDs and 2,4-dihydroxybenzoic acid. (D) The UV–Vis absorption pattern of N-GCDs and 2,4-dihydroxybenzoic acid. (E) Normalized fluorescence spectra of N-GCDs in the H2O/DMSO mixed solvents at different ratios (from left to right: pure H2O, 1:9, 2:8, 3:7, 4:6, 5:5, 6:4, 7:3, 8:2, 9:1, pure DMSO) and (F) corresponding fluorescence photographs under the UV lamp (λex = 365 nm).

(A) TEM and (B) HRTEM image of BSA-AuNCs. (C) The UV–Vis absorption pattern of BSA-AuNCs and BSA. Fluorescence spectra (D) of BSA-AuNCs in the H2O/DMSO mixed solvents at different ratios (from a-j: pure H2O, 1:9, 2:8, 3:7, 4:6, 5:5, 6:4, 7:3, 8:2, 9:1) and corresponding photographs (G) under the UV lamp at 365 nm. Fluorescence spectra (E) of BSA-AuNCs in different pH solutions and corresponding photographs (H) under the UV lamp at 365 nm. (F) The relationship between fluorescence intensity of BSA-AuNCs and pH.

(A) Solidification state of the H2O/DMSO mixed solvents at different ratios (4 °C). (B) Melting state of the H2O/DMSO mixed solvents at different ratios (6 °C). (C) Solidification state of the H2O/DMSO mixed solvents at different ratios (−20 °C). (D) Melting state of the H2O/DMSO mixed solvents at different ratios (−18 °C).

(A, D, G) Fluorescence spectra (from up to down: 0 min, 10 min, 20 min, 30 min, 40 min, 50 min, 1 h, 1.5 h, 2 h, 5 h, 9 h, 12 h, 24 h), (B, E, H) the corresponding fluorescence intensity ratio (F500 nm/F615 nm) and (C, F, I) fluorescence photographs (from left to right: 0 min, 30 min, 1 h, 9 h) of the N-GCDs/BSA-AuNCs mixture (A, B, C), the N-GCDs (D, E, F) and the BSA-AuNCs (G, H, I) solution with time after the addition of 0.025 H2O/DMSO mixed solvents. (J) With the aid of the UV lamp (λex = 365 nm), the fluorescence photograph of the N-GCDs/BSA-AuNCs/PVA-SA hydrogel (left) and after treating with 0.025 H2O/DMSO mixture (middle) and 0.135 H2O/DMSO mixture (right), respectively.

(A) Schematic diagram of the actual monitoring of fish freshness by the N-GCDs/BSA-AuNCs/PVA-SA fluorescent hydrogel. (B) Color and fluorescence intensity changes of the N-GCDs/BSA-AuNCs/PVA-SA fluorescent hydrogel in response to spoiling fish sample at room temperature. (C) The plot of ΔE values of fluorescent hydrogel versus the storage time. (D) ΔE values of the fluorescent hydrogel in response to TVB-N level at room temperature. (E) Relationship and linear correlation between TVB-N and ΔE values at room temperature.
(A) Preparation process of the N-GCDs/BSA-AuNCs/PVA-SA fluorescent hydrogel. (B) Schematic diagram of the gel for monitoring the temperature and freshness.

head.ce:title: Continuous monitoring of temperature and freshness in cold chain transport based on the dual-responsive fluorescent hydrogel

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Biobased Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education, College of Materials and Energy, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Biobased Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory for Biobased Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education, College of Materials and Energy, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Technology, Henan Agricultural University, Zhengzhou 450003, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Technology, Henan Agricultural University, Zhengzhou 450003, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Food Quality and Safety, College of Food Science, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Food Quality and Safety

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Food Quality and Safety, College of Food Science, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: They equally contributed to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Comprehensive attention should be paid to the potential food spoilage in food transport. However, there is a problem of freshness destruction by repeated freezing and thawing during the cold chain transport. Herein, a fluorescent hydrogel with N-doped green-emitting carbon dots (N-GCDs), bovine serum albumin-gold nanoclusters (BSA-AuNCs) as fluorescent probes and polyvinyl alcohol-sodium alginate hydrogel as carrier matrix was developed to continuously detect temperature and freshness. Due to the solvatochromic effect of N-GCDs, when the temperature surpassed the threshold, the mixture of water and dimethyl sulfoxide underwent a phase transition and melted into the gel, changing the fluorescence color to realize the temperature monitoring. Then, due to the pH effect of BSA-AuNCs, the gel could respond to pH changes in food deterioration to monitor the food freshness. Thus, the changes of both fluorescence color and intensity of the hydrogel provides a new method for visual and portable authenticity of food freshness.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the stringent requirements for food safety, the rapid and real-time freshness monitoring has become urgent and indispensable, especially for meat foods that are rich in protein and unsaturated fatty acids. The freshness indicator is a simple and efficient smart packaging technology that can directly and scientifically indicate the freshness of food without complex operations. The cold chain is mainly responsible for the preservation and transportation of perishable products, which can inhibit the propagation of microorganisms and endogenous enzyme activities in meat products by maintaining proper temperatures (Maiorino et al., 2021; Huang et al., 2023). However, there is a problem of freshness destruction by repeated freezing and thawing during the cold chain transport. In order to ensure the authenticity of the food freshness, the real-time detection of temperature and biogenic amines released during food spoilage is an important task (Li et al., 2022; Jia et al., 2023).

At present, conventional temperature monitoring indicators include radio frequency identification tags (Fathi et al., 2020) and time–temperature indicators (Gao et al., 2020). However, these indicators have some drawbacks, such as high equipment cost, fragility, reliance on batteries, and insufficient monitoring range. To solve the difficulty of above conventional indicators, the solvent discoloration temperature indicators have caused the attention. Usually, such indicator is mainly composed of two parts, where one is the mixture formed by two or more different solvents with the melting point at the threshold temperature, and the other is the fluorescent material with a solvatochromic effect as a response signal. When the temperature exceeds the threshold temperature, the mixture that originally exists as a solid state melts and comes into contact with the material due to a solvatochromic effect, resulting in a fluorescence change, thus the temperature monitoring is realized (Navrotskaya et al., 2022). In food spoilage, proteins, carbohydrates, fats, and other substances in food are metabolized and decomposed by microorganisms, resulting in adverse byproducts such as histamine (Li et al., 2021), hydrogen sulfide (Kwon and Ko, 2022) and volatile base total nitrogen (Zhang et al., 2023). These byproducts can alter the appearance, texture, taste, and odor of food, making it unfit to eat or unhealthy (Claudia et al., 2019). So far, many methods, including hyperspectral imaging (Shao et al., 2023), electrochemical methods (Li et al., 2019) and chromatography (Cavanna et al., 2019), have been used to monitor food freshness. However, disadvantages such as expensive equipment and complex sample pretreatment are noticeable. Therefore, it is urgent and essential to develop cost-effective, fast, and accurate methods to monitor the freshness of food.

Recently, fluorescence-based detection methods have attracted extensive attention due to the simple instrumentation, short response time and high sensitivity (Sharma et al., 2021). However, some drawbacks can’t be ignored. First, the dispersion of fluorescent probes in aqueous solutions sometimes faces the uncontrolled aggregation, thus the sensing stability is influenced. Second, the color of complex solutions including target molecules can hinder the visual monitoring of fluorescence. Finally, the difficulty in separating the fluorescent probe from the solution limits its recycling (Li et al., 2020). Thus, various solid-state sensing materials have been extensively developed. Especially, flexible fluorescent hydrogels have attracted increasing attention (Wei et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2021). The cross-linked network of hydrogels not only maintains structural stability and allows the target to permeate in the porous network (Zhang et al., 2019), but also can keep interferents larger than the pore size of the hydrogels out of the polymer network (Huang et al., 2021a, 2021b), enhancing the stability, sensitivity and anti-interference ability of the sensors. In addition, the hydrogels have excellent flexibility and mechanical stability, which make the sensors easy to carry and less likely to be damaged. In view of the advantages of fast response, good stability, high selectivity and portability, fluorescent hydrogels have been widely used in biomedicine, information coding, optical sensors and other fields. For instance, Kang et al. synthesized the RhB@Tb(BTC) dual-emission fluorescence probe using Tb(BTC) as the ligand and rhodamine B as the guest molecule by the “one-pot method” for the detection of temperature and antibiotics (Kang et al., 2023). In addition, since amines can mediate the generation of photoluminescent polymer carbon dots by β-d-glucose pentaacetate, Zhang et al. constructed the β-d-glucose pentaacetate agarose hydrogels for the highly quantitative and sensitive monitoring of shrimp amine vapor and visual detection of freshness (Zhang et al., 2022c).

In this work, the N-GCDs/BSA-AuNCs/PVA-SA fluorescent hydrogel was constructed using N-doped green-emitting carbon dots (N-GCDs), bovine serum albumin-gold nanoclusters (BSA-AuNCs) as the fluorescent materials and polyvinyl alcohol-sodium alginate (PVA-SA) hydrogel as the carrier matrix. Since N-GCDs had a solvatochromic effect (green fluorescence in water and blue fluorescence in dimethylsulfoxide) and BSA-AuNCs remained the red fluorescence, when the temperature control system of the cold chain transport failed and thus the temperature was above the threshold temperature, the mixture of water and dimethylsulfoxide (H2O/DMSO), which originally existed as a solid state, melted into the gel to quench the fluorescence of N-GCDs and induced the fluorescence color change of the gel. As a temperature indicator of solvent discoloration, the fluorescence color of hydrogel changed from yellow to pink, which realized the temperature monitoring in the process of transportation. When the temperature surpassed 4 °C and gradually rose to room temperature, the speed of food spoilage was significantly accelerated. The fluorescent hydrogel could further achieve the detection of food freshness because the BSA-AuNCs inside were sensitive to pH, which would monitor the pH change during the food spoilage process. In summary, the N-GCDs/BSA-AuNCs/PVA-SA fluorescent hydrogel exhibited the solvatochromic effect and pH sensitivity, which could realize the continuous monitoring of temperature change and food freshness during cold chain transport.

HAuCl4 was from Shenyang Jinke reagent factory. Bovine serum albumin (BSA), NaOH, 2,4-dihydroxybenzoic acid, ethylenediamine, ethylene glycol, polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and sodium alginate (SA) were from Aladdin biological technology Co. Ltd, Shanghai. Toluene, ethyl acetate, acetone, 2-propanol, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), ethanol, CaCl2 and sulfuric acid were from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Factory.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai 12, FEI, Netherlands) was used to characterize the morphologies of the materials, while a fluorescence spectrometer (F-7000, Hitachi, Japan) was used to record the fluorescence spectra. In addition, Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Nicolet IS 10, Thermo Fisher, America) was used to characterize the functional groups, while the UV–visible spectrophotometer (Evolution 220, Thermo Fisher, America) was used to record the absorption spectrum.

The glasswares for the preparation of BSA-AuNCs were immersed in aqua regia and cleaned with deionized water. First, 10 mL of 50 mg·mL−1 bovine serum albumin solution was mixed with 10 mL of 10 mM HAuCl4 solution under vigorous stirring. After reacting 2 min and adding another 1 mL of 1 M NaOH, the water bath was heated to 37 °C for 12 h, thus a reddish-brown solution was obtained, transferred to a dialysis bag and dialyzed for 48 h. Finally, the dialysis-purified BSA-AuNCs solution was stored at 4 °C.

Firstly, 0.5 g 2,4-dihydroxybenzoic acid was weighed into a beaker, then 30 mL deionized water and 1 mL ethylenediamine were added, ultrasonically dissolved and mixed evenly. After transferring the mixture to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, it was heated at 190 °C for 15 h and then cooled naturally. Then, the obtained yellowish-brown solution was poured into a round-bottomed flask and refluxed at 80 °C for 48 h. The color of the solution turned reddish-brown, indicating that N-GCDs were successfully synthesized. After the N-GCDs solution was centrifuged several times at 10,000 rpm for 15 min, the supernatant was collected and filtered with a 0.22 μM membrane. Finally, the filtrate was transferred to the rotary evaporator to remove the solvent, while the N-GCDs were dispersed in anhydrous ethanol and dried at 70 °C, thus the N-GCDs powder was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the N-GCDs/BSA-AuNCs/PVA-SA fluorescent hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: First, 0.5 g PVA was completely dissolved in 10 mL deionized water at 95 °C. After cooling to room temperature, 0.5 g sodium alginate powder, 8.889 mL 2 g·L−1 N-GCDs, and 1.111 mL BSA-AuNCs were added into the PVA solution and stirred overnight to obtain a uniform solution. Then, the mixture was poured into a culture dish, frozen in a refrigerator at −20 °C for 22 h, and thawed at room temperature for 2 h. The repetition of such frozen-thaw process at least 3 cycles can effectively realize physical cross-linking. Finally, the 4 % CaCl2 solution was poured into the culture dish to form cross-linked hydrogel. After soaking for 4 h, the gel was repeatedly rinsed with deionized water to remove the excess CaCl2 solution. So far, the N-GCDs/BSA-AuNCs/PVA-SA fluorescent hydrogel was successfully prepared. In addition, the PVA-SA hydrogel was obtained by only adding 10.0 mL of deionized water, while the N-GCDs/PVA-SA hydrogel or the BSA-AuNCs/PVA-SA hydrogel was obtained by only adding the same volume of N-GCDs or BSA-AuNCs and 8.889 mL of deionized water. The various gels were stored at 4 °C for subsequent use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selection of H2O/DMSO mixture ratio to the threshold temperature
ce:section.ce:para: A series of H2O/DMSO mixtures with different proportions were prepared and placed in the refrigerator. The ratio of the mixture that was solid at 4 °C and melted to liquid quickly above 4 °C was used as the solvent ratio of discoloration indicator at 4 °C. Similarly, the ratio of the mixture corresponding to a threshold temperature of −20 °C was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the feasibility of monitoring fish freshness with the fluorescent hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: The perch fish was killed, boned, skinned and finally divided into portions by the market vendors. Then, we purchased them as deceased samples and placed in containers sealed with transparent cling film to monitor freshness. The whole process, from slaughter on-site to processing into test samples, was controlled within 45 min to ensure the freshness of the fish.

When the temperature was higher than 4 °C and gradually climbed to room temperature (25 °C), the H2O/DMSO mixture melted into liquid and flowed into the fluorescent hydrogel, thus the feasibility of the fluorescent hydrogel for monitoring fish freshness was explored. The N-GCDs/BSA-AuNCs/PVA-SA fluorescent hydrogel and the H2O/DMSO mixture at a ratio of 0.025 were placed in a disposable lunch box together with the fish and sealed with cling film for 24 h at room temperature to observe the changes in fluorescence color of the gel.

At the same time, the TVB-N values of the corresponding fish samples were measured every 3 h based on the Chinese standard (GB 5009.228-2016). The CIE Lab (International Commission on Illumination L, a, and b values) coordinates were determined by using Photoshop software to process fluorescence photos of the gel, and then put into a specific formula to calculate the total color difference (ΔE) of the gel as follows (Liu et al., 2022):
Δ E =
Δ L
2
+
Δ a
2
+
Δ b
2
where a is the deviation from green to red, b is the deviation from blue to yellow, and L is the lightness.

As in Scheme 1 A, the fluorescent hydrogel was prepared by decorating the N-GCDs and BSA-AuNCs in PVA-SA by the consecutive frozen-thaw process and the cross-linking with Ca2+. Scheme 1B was the schematic diagram of the fluorescent hydrogel for the monitoring of temperature and freshness during cold chain transport. When the temperature control system failed during cold chain transport and the temperature (T) exceeded the threshold temperature (Tt), the H2O/DMSO mixture with a melting point of Tt melted into the gel. The fluorescence of N-GCDs was quenched by DMSO, while the fluorescence of BSA-AuNCs was unaffected, causing the fluorescence of the gel change from yellow to pink and thus realizing the temperature detection. Then, as fish spoilage increased, the concentration of volatile amines increased accordingly and the pH inside the gel rose, which made the pink fluorescence of the gel weaken or even quench, thus realizing the monitoring of freshness. Therefore, the fluorescent hydrogel could realize continuous monitoring of temperature and freshness during cold chain transport.

Fig. 1 A was the TEM image of N-GCDs, while Fig. 1B was the HRTEM image of N-GCDs, showing that N-GCDs had obvious lattice fringes with a spacing of about 0.24 nm, which was consistent with that reported in the literature (Moniruzzaman and Kim, 2019). As in Fig. 1C, compared with the raw material 2,4-dihydroxybenzoic acid, N-GCDs showed a stretching-vibration peak at 1600 cm−1 from CN/CC in the infrared spectrum, which was formed by the reaction between 2,4-dihydroxybenzoic acid and ethylenediamine, indicating the successful preparation of N-GCDs (Moniruzzaman and Kim, 2019). Fig. 1D was the UV–Vis spectrum of 2,4-dihydroxybenzoic acid and N-GCDs. The N-GCDs exhibited a wide band centered at 420 nm, which was produced by the n-π* transition of typical surface functional groups (carboxyl and/or CN bonds), causing the intense green emission of N-GCDs (Yang et al., 2023; Shamsipur et al., 2018). Fig. S1A showed the normalized fluorescence spectra of N-GCDs in different solvents, from which it could be seen that the peak positions of each curve were different, indicating that N-GCDs were the fluorescent material with a solvatochromic effect. From left to right, the solvents were toluene, ethyl acetate, acetone, N,N-dimethylformamide, dimethyl sulfoxide, isopropanol, ethanol, ethylene glycol and water. With the increase of solvent polarity, the fluorescence peak position shifted from 430 nm to 510 nm, and the corresponding fluorescence color changed from blue to green (Fig. S1B). Fig. 1E showed the normalized fluorescence spectra of N-GCDs in the H2O/DMSO mixed solvents at different ratios, displaying that the peak positions of the fluorescence curves were different at different ratios. As the DMSO content in the mixed solvents decreased and the H2O content increased, the fluorescence peak position shifted from 470 nm to 510 nm and the fluorescence color changed from blue to green (Fig. 1F). Fig. S2A was the fluorescence spectra of N-GCDs in different pH buffers. As in Fig. S2B, when pH increased from 2 to 8, the fluorescence intensity showed an overall upward trend; however the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased when pH further increased. Fig. S2C was the corresponding fluorescence photos, showing the image changed from no fluorescence to bright green fluorescence and then to no fluorescence.

Fig. 2 A was a TEM image of BSA-AuNCs, where the black dots attached to the residual, lumpy bovine serum albumin molecules were the synthesized BSA-AuNCs. Fig. 2B was an HRTEM image of BSA-AuNCs, showing clear lattice stripes. Fig. 2C was the UV–Vis spectrum of BSA-AuNCs with a strong absorption peak at 275 nm, which was similar with earlier work (Ni et al., 2019). Fig. 2D was the fluorescence spectra of BSA-AuNCs in the H2O/DMSO mixture at different ratios. The multiple fluorescence curves only showed changes in fluorescence intensity without shifts in the positions of fluorescence peaks, while the fluorescence photo under the UV lamp did not undergo color changes (Fig. 2G), indicating that BSA-AuNCs did not have a solvatochromic effect. Fig. 2E was the fluorescence spectra of BSA-AuNCs in different pH buffers, while the corresponding fluorescence images were shown in Fig. 2H. Similarly, the fluorescence intensity tended to increase when the pH was in the range of 2–8, while the fluorescence intensity tended to decrease when the pH was in the range of 8–12.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the threshold temperature at different H2O/DMSO ratio
ce:section.ce:para: The temperature at which a crystal begins to melt is called the melting point. Various crystals with different interaction forces between particles result in different melting points. Since the H2O-DMSO system can be fully crystallized at all ratios and the interaction forces within the crystals formed are different, H2O-DMSO mixtures with different ratios displays different melting points (Rasmussen and Mackenzie, 1968; Wong et al., 2012). In this work, H2O and DMSO were used as the mixed solvent to explore the ratio of H2O and DMSO corresponding to the threshold temperature. First, as shown in Fig. 3A, a series of H2O/DMSO mixed solvents with different ratios were prepared and frozen at 4 °C. It was the case that the mixture was solidified when the ratio of H2O/DMSO in the mixture was 0.01, 0.02 and 0.025, while the mixture remained liquid when the ratio was 0.03, 0.04 and 0.07. The 4 °C-treated mixture was then removed and placed in an environment above 4 °C (approximately 6 °C), showing that the mixture with a ratio of 0.025 immediately melted and changed from a solidified state to a liquid state (Fig. 3 B). Consequently, a mixture of H2O and DMSO with a proportion of 0.025 was chosen as the indicator solvent at the threshold temperature of 4 °C. Second, another sequence of H2O/DMSO solvents mixed with ratios of 0.10, 0.13, 0.135, 0.14, 0.15 and 0.17 was formulated and frozen at −20 °C respectively, as shown in Fig. 3C, and it was found that the mixture changed from liquid to solid when the ratio of H2O/DMSO was 0.10, 0.13 and 0.135, while the mixture remained liquid when the ratio was 0.14, 0.15 and 0.17. The mixtures were then taken out of −20 °C and exposed to an environment higher than −20 °C (approximately −18 °C), showing that the mixture with a ratio of 0.135 instantly melted from a condensed solid to a flowing liquid state (Fig. 3D). As a result, a mixture of H2O and DMSO at the ratio of 0.135 was selected as the indicator solvent for the threshold temperature of −20 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Monitoring temperature changes based on the N-GCDs/BSA-AuNCs/PVA-SA fluorescent hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: The configured N-GCDs/BSA-AuNCs mixed solution was equally divided into three parts, followed by adding the same volume of H2O, H2O/DMSO mixture at the ratio of 0.025, and H2O/DMSO mixture at the ratio of 0.135, respectively. Moreover, the fluorescence spectra and fluorescence color changes of the mixed solutions after the addition of the H2O/DMSO mixture with different ratios were observed by using the mixed solution with the addition of pure H2O as a reference.

In Fig. S3, compared with the mixed solution with pure H2O, the curves of mixed solution with H2O/DMSO mixture was offset at the position of fluorescence emission peak of N-GCDs and the fluorescence intensity decreased significantly. However, the curve of mixed solution with pure H2O and the curves of mixed solution with H2O/DMSO mixture were overlapped at the peak position of BSA-AuNCs, which indicated that the fluorescence emission peak position and fluorescence intensity of BSA-AuNCs remained almost unchanged. Due to the solvatochromic effect of N-GCDs, the position and intensity of its peak were changed when DMSO was introduced into the pure aqueous solution, whereas the BSA-AuNCs did not respond to DMSO. Correspondingly, when the series of solutions were exposed to the UV lamp, it was observed that the mixed solution with pure H2O and the mixed solutions with H2O/DMSO mixture presented different fluorescence colors.

To achieve a more pronounced and intuitive visual color change, the ratio between N-GCDs and BSA-AuNCs was modulated as shown in Fig. S3. When the ratio of N-GCDs to BSA-AuNCs was set at 1:1, 2:1 and 4:1, the fluorescence colors of the mixed solutions corresponding to pure H2O, 0.025 H2O/DMSO and 0.135 H2O/DMSO were all homochromatic. When the ratio of N-GCDs and BSA-AuNCs was 6:1, 8:1, 16:1, 24:1, the fluorescence colors of the mixed solutions corresponding to pure H2O, 0.025 H2O/DMSO and 0.135 H2O/DMSO achieved variations between different homochromatic families. Since the fluorescence color changes between different color systems were more vivid at the visual level, the mixed solution of N-GCDs and BSA-AuNCs with a ratio higher than 4:1 was selected as an alternative. In addition, it was observed that when the ratio between N-GCDs and BSA-AuNCs was 16:1 and 24:1, the fluorescence intensity of the mixed solution after the addition of H2O/DMSO mixed solvent was weaker. Considering diminished contrast between the fluorescence colors and the corresponding poor visual identification, an optimal ratio of 8:1 between N-GCDs and BSA-AuNCs was selected.

Then, the influence of the time on the solvatochromic effect of N-GCDs was investigated. Fig. 4 A was the influence of the time on the fluorescence spectroscopy of the mixture of N-GCDs/BSA-AuNCs after the addition of H2O/DMSO. As in Fig. 4B, the fluorescence intensity ratio (F500 nm/F615 nm) of the N-GCDs/BSA-AuNCs gradually decreased due to the longer time. When the time reached 9 h, the fluorescence intensity ratio (F500 nm/F615 nm) remained constant, so 9 h was selected as the optimum incubation time. Fig. 4C was the corresponding photos of the N-GCDs/BSA-AuNCs solution after treating for 0 min, 30 min, 1 h, 9 h under the UV lamp, showing a fluorescence color change from bright yellow to bright red. Furthermore, two solutions containing only N-GCDs or only BSA-AuNCS were prepared to investigate the fluorescence discoloration. According to Fig. 4D, the peak position of the N-GCDs gradually shifted slightly with increasing time, while the fluorescence intensity decreased significantly (Fig. 4E), and the solution fluorescence color changed from bright green to weak blue-green (Fig. 4F). However, in Fig. 4G, the multiple fluorescence curves of BSA-AuNCs almost completely overlapped, the peak position and intensity of BSA-AuNCs did not change with increasing time (Fig. 4H), and the fluorescence color remained bright red (Fig. 4I). Therefore, it was further demonstrated that the changes in fluorescence spectra and fluorescence colors of the mixed solutions were caused by the N-GCDs with a solvatochromic effect.

By adjusting different ratios of N-GCDs and BSA-AuNCs, the possibility of monitoring temperature based on the changes in solution fluorescence color was investigated. Fig. S4A was the fluorescence spectra of solutions obtained with pure water mixed with different ratios of N-GCDs/BSA-AuNCs. It was found that as the N-GCDs/BSA-AuNCs ratio increased, the fluorescence intensity of N-GCDs increased and the fluorescence intensity of BSA-AuNCs decreased, while the corresponding fluorescence color of the solutions transitioned from the initial orange-red to an intermediate yellow color and finally a green color (Fig. S4D). Fig. S4B and Fig. S4C showed two sets of mixed solutions with different N-GCDs/BSA-AuNCs ratios by adding solvents of 0.025 H2O/DMSO and 0.135 H2O/DMSO, respectively, displaying that the fluorescence intensity of N-GCDs in both sets of fluorescence spectra increased continuously as the ratio of N-GCDs/BSA-AuNCs increased, while the fluorescence intensity of BSA-AuNCs both kept diminishing, and thus the fluorescence color changed from red to pink (Fig. S4E and Fig. S4F). Since the N-GCDs and BSA-AuNCs had different fluorescence emission peaks and exhibited different colors of fluorescence, thus the ratio between N-GCDs and BSA-AuNCs could be adjusted to obtain a variety of fluorescence color, making it possible to detect temperature based on the visual and intuitive changes in fluorescence color.

As a natural polymer of glyoxalic acid (gulo-gulonic acid and mannuronic acid), sodium alginate can form hydrogels with various divalent cations such as calcium ions by ionic cross-linking (Ramdhan et al., 2020) with the advantages of being environmentally friendly, easy to process and low cost (Shen et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2021). Polyvinyl alcohol is a water-soluble, biodegradable and easily modified polymer with excellent mechanical properties, good biosafety and no toxicity or side effects on humans(Chopra et al., 2022). Nowadays, several studies were relative with the application of polyvinyl alcohol-sodium alginate hydrogels in organic adsorption (Zhang et al., 2022b), water purification (Zhang et al., 2022a) and wound healing (Niranjan et al., 2019). Herein, polyvinyl alcohol-sodium alginate interpenetrating network gel was obtained by cyclic freeze–thaw and ionic cross-linking to perform carriers of fluorescent hydrogel sensors for temperature change and food freshness monitoring. From the previous description, the N-GCDs/BSA-AuNCs/PVA-SA hydrogel was prepared by selecting an 8:1 ratio of N-GCDs/BSA-AuNCs mixture. As in Fig. S5A, the blank hydrogel, N-GCDs/BSA-AuNCs/PVA-SA hydrogel, N-GCDs/PVA-SA hydrogel and BSA-AuNCs/PVA-SA hydrogel, showed no fluorescence, yellow fluorescence, green fluorescence and red fluorescence under the UV lamp accordingly. Then, the gel blocks were immersed in 0.025 H2O/DMSO solvent mixture and 0.135 H2O/DMSO solvent mixture, respectively. After a period of time, the gel blocks were taken out and their fluorescence colors were observed under the UV lamp. Taking the original gel as a reference, it was found that the immersed hydrogel changed from yellow to pink (Fig. 4J), which was consistent with the experimental results in the solution state, indicating that the combination of N-GCDs/BSA-AuNCs/PVA-SA hydrogel with H2O/DMSO mixed solvent could realize the visual monitoring of temperature changes.

In addition, the process and time required for the hydrogel fluorescence color to change after the H2O/DMSO solvent mixture flowed into the N-GCDs/BSA-AuNCs/PVA-SA hydrogel were also explored. As shown in Fig. S5B, when the H2O/DMSO mixture melted into the solution at room temperature and just started to enter the hydrogel, the hydrogel showed the original yellow fluorescence. Then, due to the high absorption of the hydrogel's porous structure, the solution can diffuse into the hydrogel through the pore channels, causing the hydrogel glowing a little light red fluorescence at the time of 15 min. Finally, when the time reached 30 min, the entire hydrogel was filled with the H2O/DMSO solvent mixture, the green fluorescence of the N-GCDs was quenched, resulting in the pink fluorescence of the gel.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Monitoring the fish freshness by the N-GCDs/BSA-AuNCs/PVA-SA fluorescent hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: Owing to its balanced nutrition, flavor and protein content, fish has become the essential daily food (Liu et al., 2020a). However, fish is a highly perishable food with a short shelf life, making monitoring its freshness very important for consumers, retailers and the industry. The main causes of fish spoilage are enzymatic reactions and microbial metabolism (Hasanah et al., 2019). The volatile amines produced during fish spoilage can change the pH of the microenvironment, therefore fish freshness can be indirectly detected by monitoring changes in pH (Tavakoli et al., 2023).

Firstly, the stability of the N-GCDs/BSA-AuNCs/PVA-SA fluorescent hydrogel at 4 °C was studied. As shown in Fig. S6A, when stored at 4 °C for about a month, the fluorescence intensity of the gel decreased compared to the initial fluorescence intensity. Secondly, to investigate the thermal stability of the N-GCDs/BSA-AuNCs/PVA-SA fluorescent hydrogel, the H2O/DMSO solvent-soaked gel blocks were placed in a 30 °C oven and then removed after 24 h, showing that the gel fluorescence color and intensity did not change significantly (Fig. S6B), thus the gel could be used for monitoring fish spoilage at room temperature. Thirdly, the fish, the N-GCDs/BSA-AuNCs/PVA-SA fluorescent hydrogel and the H2O/DMSO mixture were placed together in a food preservation box at room temperature. As shown in Fig. S6C, as time increased, the 0.025 H2O/DMSO mixture melted from a solid to a liquid state and flowed into the gel, causing the gel fluorescence color to change from the original yellow to pink, and then gradually decrease with increasing fish spoilage. After 24 h later, the gel fluorescence was completely burnt off. In an alkaline environment, the fluorescence intensity of BSA-AuNCs gradually decreased with increasing pH (Fig. 2F), thus volatile amines in the fish spoilage can cause the weakening and even sudden extinction of the BSA-AuNCs fluorescence. In addition, by using water instead of fish (Fig. S6D), it could be observed that the gel fluorescence color remained unchanged after 24 h, suggesting that the volatile amines from fish spoilage entered the gel and changed the internal pH to result in the quench of gel fluorescence, rather than by the exchange of water formed by the cooling of water vapor during spoilage exotherm with the solution inside the gel. In this way, the N-GCDs/BSA-AuNCs/PVA-SA fluorescent hydrogel was feasible for fish freshness monitoring. Finally, Fig. 5 A showed a schematic diagram of the actual monitoring of fish freshness by the N-GCDs/BSA-AuNCs/PVA-SA fluorescent hydrogel, where the fish was placed in a freshness box and the gel, and the H2O/DMSO mixture placed in a curved recess at the top of the box. When the temperature exceeded the threshold temperature, the H2O/DMSO mixture quickly melted into the gel, making the fluorescence color change to pink, then the fish gradually spoilt and the volatile amines entered the gel, altering the pH inside the gel and weakening the gel fluorescence until it was completely quenched, realizing the monitoring of fish freshness.

TVB-N is the alkaline compound including ammonia (NH3), dimethylamine (C2H7N) and trimethylamine (C3H9N) produced by the degradation of proteins and other nitrogenous substances in animal food (Dudnyk et al, 2018; Ezati et al, 2019; Lee et al, 2019). As the degree of meat spoilage increases, the concentration of TVB-N increases accordingly. Therefore, TVB-N can be used as an important freshness indicator for meat safety evaluation (Bekhit et al., 2021). Herein, the fish spoilage experiments were performed at room temperature and the corresponding fluorescence changes of the hydrogel were recorded, while the content of TVB-N was also measured.

In Fig. S7, the TVB-N content of the fish increased with a longer storage time, and the freshness of the fish gradually decreased. By the time longer for 18 h, the acceleration of TVB-N content increased significantly to 20.79 mg/100 g, which was slightly higher than the consumption limit of 20 mg/100 g for freshwater fish (GB2733-2015), indicating that the fish sample was no longer edible. The N-GCDs/BSA-AuNCs/PVA-SA hydrogel displayed visible fluorescence signal changes in response to TVB-N during fish spoilage under the UV lamp (Fig. 5B), showing the gel fluorescence gradually diminished as the level of TVB-N content increased, while the gel fluorescence diminished more significantly at a TVB-N value of 20.79 mg/100 g.

To further validate the reliability of the N-GCDs/BSA-AuNCs/PVA-SA fluorescent hydrogel for monitoring fish freshness, the color difference value ΔE, that can indicate the magnitude of the difference between the two colors on the CIE Lab scale, was used to investigate the relationship between the change in the gel fluorescence and the spoilage process. The relationship between ΔE values of fluorescent hydrogel and the storage time of fish samples at room temperature was displayed in Fig. 5C. As the time increased, the ΔE value increased accordingly, indicating that the color value of the gel was positively correlated with the storage time. In Fig. 5D, an increase in ΔE values was observed with increasing TVB-N content. If the TVB-N was above 20 mg/100 g, the corresponding ΔE value was approximately 10. Normally, when the ΔE value was larger than 5, the color could be recognized and detected (Liu et al., 2020b), so it was possible to identify changes in the gel fluorescence color to determine whether the fish had reached its consumption limit. As in Fig. 5E, when the TVB-N concentration ranged from 11.68 to 27.69 mg/100 g, a linear relationship between the ΔE value (y) with the TVB-N concentration (x) was discovered as y = 0.79x-6.24 (R2 = 0.998). Therefore, quantitative analysis of TVB-N could be achieved using the change in the gel fluorescence signal. Furthermore, when the TVB-N value reached the edible limit of 20 mg/100 g, the corresponding ΔE value in the CIE Lab method was 9.56 according to the linear equation. Thus, when the ΔE value of the fish sample exceeded 9.56, the fish was spoiled and inedible. In summary, the fluorescent hydrogel combined with the CIE Lab analysis method could monitor the freshness changes of fish and identify whether the fish was edible.

In this work, the novel N-GCDs/BSA-AuNCs/PVA-SA fluorescent hydrogel was constructed to the continuous monitoring of temperature and freshness in food cold chain transport. Based on the solvent-chromic temperature property, the temperature monitoring can be realized by the fluorescent color change due to the melting of the solvent into the gel. In addition, the pH effect of the N-GCDs/BSA-AuNCs/PVA-SA hydrogel allowed further monitoring the food freshness after temperature runaway based on the changes in fluorescence intensity. In summary, the new method developed upon the N-GCDs/BSA-AuNCs/PVA-SA hydrogel not only provides a new idea for visual and portable on-site authenticity of the food freshness, but also plays great importance for ensuring food safety.

Yiran Cao: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Mengting Chen: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Jialin Li: Methodology, Investigation. Weipeng Liu: Investigation, Methodology. Hongshuai Zhu: Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization, Funding acquisition. Yingju Liu: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22274059, 32170100), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2022M711058), and Natural Science Foundation of Henan Province (232300420195).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.137981.
